CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Artt, literature, ‘‘ Hungarianism,”” landlordism, tin
pikery, cattle driving, comedy and tragedy are mixed
up all of a heap in a little piece of news that comes to
us from the County of Galway. As our readers know,
a more or less ‘“ Hungarian '’ local paper, the ‘ Ennis-
corthy Lcho,” is an out-and-out advocate of cattle
driving ; at a recent ‘ Hungarian '’ meeting in Dublin
one of the speakers i8 reported to have applauded cattle
driving ; it is also known that one of the Vice-Presidents
of the so-called ‘‘ National Council,”” Mr. John Sweet-
man, is against cattle driving; Mr. Sweetman is also
against passively resisting the Income-tax collector, for,
as he points out, his furniture would be seized! What
of Mr. Edward Martyn, full President of the uncon-
sciously comical body - whose bitterest opponents
could not have wished them worse than that Mr. James
Brady, the eminent police court solicitor, should have
joined their ranks?

As people with a keen sense of humour, we
openly confess that the news from Gort is to

us as champagne to a Dublin ‘ Corinthian.”” Edward
VIIL. owns some land in the county of Galway, and
Edward VIIL. is President of that conglomeration of
British Civil Servants, ex-tin pikers, fanatics, and boys
without a sense of humour, who go, with other types,
to make up the Green Hungarian Band. Now, Mr.
Tdward Martyn, besides being a member of the Kildare
Street Club is, as we have said, a landowner in Co.
Galway, as Mr. John Sweetman is in Meath. There is
a gentleman named Mr. Persse, who is brother to one
of the chief workers and figures in contemporary litera-
ture and drama in Ireland, Lady Gregory. Of course
Lady Gregory has really nothing to do with the mabter,
but the fact that such a distinguished figure in artistic
Ireland is brother to the Mr. Persse concerned adds
piquancy to the comedy. Mr. Persse is, we understand,
under police protection! He is agent of one Lord
Geugh, whoever he is. The Persse family, at one time
any way, were famous, we are told, for their soup schools
and proselytising energy. The family have that tradi-
tion to live down. We mention it now merely as an
interesting matter of history. Mr. Persse has cattle;
Mr. Edward Martyn, President of the °‘National
Council,”” has land. Our information is that the Pre-
sident of the ‘‘ National Council ”’ has repeatedly re-
fused to give this farm for division amongst the poor
people of the district. The President of the Green
Hungarian Band prefers to let it on the eleven months®
system to a man under police protection, Mr. Persse,
agent to Lord Gough, and a man who has been described
to us—with what amount of justification, if any, we
cannot say, as we are utter strangers to the locality—
as ‘““one of the most tyrannical land sharks in Con-
nacht.””  Well, the cattle of this alleged ‘“land shark **
were driven off Mr. Edward Martyn’s land lasb Satur-
day night!

If it be true that ‘“cattle driving”’ is a
‘“ Hungarian ’’ weapon, then, so far as Edward VIII.
is concerned, it may be said that Hungarianism, like
curses, comes home to roost! It is hard to realise it—
there has been a cattle drive on the land of Mr. Edward
Martyn, President of the ‘‘ National Council *’! What
does the boy Arthur think of it? Surely even a pas-
sion for statistics must cool at the dread tidings thai
there has been a ‘‘ cattle drive’ from Mr. Edward
Martyn’s land, the cattle being the property. of a police-
protected landlord’s agent, an alleged ““land shark ’’!
What - will Alderman Kelly do now? Will he go to
Tulira Castle and lay his tin-sword at the feet of Edward
VIII., exclaiming, ‘“ My liege, T can no longer salute
thee king.”” What does the Irish “ Deak’’ and the
TIrish ““ Kossuth ”’ think of it? There has been a -attle
drive on the estate of their President! What of Mr.
John Sweetman? Has he no cattle, or does he let his
land to the cattle of other people? And “cattle
driving "’ is claimed as a ‘‘ Hungarian’’ policy by
somo. We think that Lady Gregory should attempt a
comedy on this incident and on the situation which
surrounds it. Tt is full of comedy. ‘The Cattle Drive
of Tulira’’ would draw crowded houses to the Abbey
Theatre for months, and it would, or should, if pro-
perly done, be a healing criticism. of a phase of Irish
life. Lady Gregory would be just the person to write
the play, for as the cattle belonged to her brother,
and the land to Mr. Edward Martyn, the President of
the ‘‘ National Council,”” she would be in an excellent
position to view the whole situation in a comic light.

The poor guarantors of Humphrey’s Dump are reci-
pients of a sad Christmas Box. It appears that in
order to pull down interest expenses at the rate of

t 7 per cent. on their indebtedness, the Bank of
and has offered: facilities to guarantors -to pay up
| per cent.. of their guarantee at once!! Mr. W. F.
Pen,nehy, who was editor of the ‘‘ Independent ’’ when
1t ‘as the daily ‘‘ Platitude,’”’ is now secretary of the
~ Finance and General Purposes Committee of Humph-
- rey’s Dump (Failure).

In the leading article in the ‘‘ Independent’ of
Monday last we read, ‘ Ireland’s interest is our whole
and sole concern.” Well, we despise the ‘‘ Freeman’’
but we distrust the ‘‘Independent.’”” Who and what
is the ‘‘Independent’? It foisted that clectric
lighted calamity, the so-called ‘‘ International Exhibi-
i tion,”” on this sore-pressed country. Even when the

— Tramway Company has paid up its proportion of the
guarantee and its extra expenses, wo don’t know that
1t will show such a great, surplus of profits as a result
of Humphrey’s Dump.

What or who does the ‘“Independent’” stand for?
If Mr. Ginnell is to be deposed as national leader of
‘the Irish race at home, is that any reason that Mr.
Murphy’s ¢ Independent,’’ should be pushed into that
position? When the warring factions unite, when the
*‘ Freeman ’’ lies down with the ‘“ Independent,’” when
Redmond, Healy, Dillon, and O’Brien go into harness,
there will be plenty of work for us to do to keep the
combination cool and: in; order !

On our way from the Winter Show at Ball’s Bridge
last week we went into Humphrey’s Dump (Failed).
There had been an auction of shrubs. It was a damp

“evening, inclined to drizzle: a lot of old dirty looking
flags drooped in sympathy with the spirits of the un-
fortunate guarantors who have to pay up. In one of
the outside refreshment saloons we saw a sight that
“would freeze the heart of any right-thinking “ bona ”’
—we saw beer pumps and no beer! There was nothing
in the place but bare shelves and the beer pumps—not
-even corked bottles of air. The baby incubator show
was still standing, but no babies. What we took to
be the Somali village, or rather the site of it, was as
a place laid waste by fire and sword. One of the
pretty bridges over the water connected with the chute
was 1n a partly dilapidated condition, and the plaster
was broken. Indeed the plaster in many other parts
was broken also, and the illusion of alabaster pillars
and so forth was shattered as. completely as the san-
guine * hopes of the unfortunate guarantors. The
Palace of Industries was a palace of desertion: a few
stands remained to emphasise the emptiness. The
stand of our friends, the Silverspring Starch Co., was
still there, but if our recollection serves us well, the
starch was gone! We hear that a great many things
were stolen from the Exhibition. The highways of

e, the Dump were badly cut up by the wheels of heavy

" vehicles that had taken away the exhibits, and wany
a place where seoinin Johnnies and simpering 1 -aidens
promenaded to the tune of Tommy {&tkins’ bands,
were now cubt up in ruts. The Machinery Hall, or
whatever it was called, the place on the right as you
enter from Donnybrook, was like a hall subsequent to
“a violent gas explosion. Its glory was gone. Altogether
this afterview of Humphrey’s Dump was a depressing
sight. Well, we fought our best against the holding of

~ this failure. Let the “ Independent’” offer what con-

. solation it can to the unfortunate guarantors.® Mr.
@innell and his cattle drivers may be one extreme: let

s not forget that the ¢ Independent,”” helped by the
Irish Times,”” thrust this costly failure into being.

Before us is the programme of a Dramatic
Entertainment that was held-in Clongowes College, on
December 8th. It is called; the “ Ticket-of-Leave Man
(adapted)’” Nothing very Irish about that, except that
an Irish patriot or two may have been at the time on
ticket-of-leave. The. first character, we presume the
““horo,”” is “ Robert Brierly (a Glossop lad)”” Glossop,
wherever it may be, is, we are sure at quite a ‘‘re-
spectable ”’ distance from Ireland, not to say from
Sallins. There are navvies in the piece, but then there
are nayvies and navvies; we take it that in Clongowes
thore is a certain ‘“ tone’’ about the English navvies.
There are a few songs on the programme, but not one
in Trvish. What ever one may think of Cattle Driving,
it is a pity that some of these nation-killing, Seoinin
breeding colleges could not be treated to a bit of the
policy of the hazel.

A reader from Belfast, in, the course of a letter,
writes:—‘‘ A few months ago I went into a shop in
Belfast and asked for the LeapEr.. ‘I haven’t got it,
sir, but T have ‘“ T. P.’s Weekly,”” maybe that will do
as well.” T fled in horror from the barbarian ,as you
may easily imagine.”’

The following resolution, received, from the Keating
Branch of the Gaelic League, was adopted by the Dublin
Corporation at its meeting held on the 9th inst., and by
the Dublin County Council at its meeting on Thursday
last :—““ That we consider the action of the National
Board, in withdrawing the fees for Irish as an ordinary
subject in the National Schools, a violation of the
settlement offered by Mr. Birrell last year; that we
advise managers and teachers of schools, and particu-
larly extern teachers employed by the Gaelic League,
to provide for the teaching of Irish within school hours ;
and that we ask the public to assist the Gaelic League
in obtaining remuneration for the teachers for this
work in accordance with the scale of fees-laid down in
Mr. Birrell’s scheme.”’

In our issue of last week, the Irish Agricultural
Wholesale Society Ltd., advertised Basic Slag, Seeds,
Feeding Stuffs, and other agricultural requirements.
In printing the advertisement an error occurred which
we now wish to correct. The address of the Society
was wrongly set up as 125. Thomas Street. The correct
address is 151 Thomas Street.

We have received a Catalogue and Price List from
Messrs. Malone Bros., Ltd., of their well-known sham-
yuck polishes, etec. The list is an extensive one, and
includes, in addition to their leading lines, such as
boot, metal and wax polishes, many other branches
of manufacture for which there should be a growing
trade in TIreland.  Amongst these we may mention
harness compo., which is extensively used in the
harness manufacturing line. The foreign article hag
had, we understand, hitherto all its own way, so that
there is plenty of room for Malone Bros, for develop-
ments in this line.

DECEMBER .21, 1907.

We have received the current issue of the C.Y.M.;
:}\J.\e contents of which are, as usual, interesting. The
notes and comments deal with various topics ranging
from Temperance to the appointment of Mr. Judge
*Bodkin. ‘Though the size of dur contemporary has been
Tecently ‘enlarged, the. price is still 1d.

There were probably some’ thousands of pounds sent
‘out of Ireland for the foreign plaster-of-Paris used in
the building of the International Exhibition. Fow-
ever, the Exhibition authorities cannot be blamed on

B this account, as Irish-made plaster-of-Paris was non-

existent when these buildings were being constructed.
It was only in May of this year that an Irish factory
for the production of such material came into being.
About that time the Dublin Plaster Company were
getting into working order, and since then, we are glad
to learn, they have been taking a substantial slice of
the market that had been a monopoly for imported
plaster. Plaster-of-Paris is an important element in
the construction of many classes of structures, so it is
easy to see the importance of this addition to our in-
dustrial assets. The Dublin Plaster Company ' have
their works at Ringsend, and manufacture various
qualities for different requirements. We were glad to
read the opinion of an expert on the merits of this
Ringsend plaster, Mr. John Ryan, the well-
known Abbey Street Contractor, who wrote highly
of the sample he had used. The plaster-work, by the
way, in St. Agatha’s Church is being done by Mr.
Ryan with this Irish plaster solely. We hope all
our readers in the architectural and building way will
lend a hand in developing this mew industry. The
agents of the Company are Messrs. Henry Wallace and
Co., 17 Westland Row, from whom particulars as to
prices, etc., may be obtained.

_ When purchasing goods as a result of advertiseinents
in the LEADER, our readers would do this paper a ‘service
by mentioning it as the source of their information.
We have ample reason to know that many already do
50, but in our large circle of readers there must be
many others who omit to mention the name of the
Leiper, when doing business with firms as ‘a result of
advertisements in its columns. Now that we have sug-
gested that references to the LEADER in this connection
would be serviceable to it, we are sure they will avail of
such opportunities in future.

‘One Sergeant Craig, a policeman, was recently ‘trans-

- ferred from Lisdoonvarna to Ennis, the ‘capital of the
Banner County of which Matty Bodkin is now judge.
Of course such an epoch-making event as the shifting
of policeman Craig from Lisdoonvarna to Ennis, could
not be allowed to take place without the hat going
round. The policeman was presented with a purse of
sovereigns and, of course, an address. The policeman’s
friends assembled in McInerney’s Hotel and gave him a

. send-off—we have not heard who paid for the drinks.
- ‘We assume there were drinks as the send-off took place
in a hotel. Probably the sergeant paid ‘and ‘some of
the ‘subscribers were no doubt at the ‘‘send-off ”’ with

a view of getting some of their own back. ‘Amongst
the policeman’s friends at the ‘‘sond-off”’ were Dr.

Wo regret to seo by the ¢ Freeman” that Mr. P. A,
McHugh, M.P., has been ill, we trust that he is fu!ly
recovered by this time. The ¢ Freeman '’ goes quite
into detail concerning the matter. Whilst only con-
valescont, the ‘‘Freeman’’ tells the public that ‘he
left for London with his daughter, and travelled phro&xgh
to London, Dover, Ostend, Brussells and Louvain with-
out stopping. Having left his daughter in the convent,
where she is being educated, he turned on his heel and
came back the same route, without rest or halt, travelled
night and day all the time in cold and stormy weather.”

It occurs to us, as the ‘‘ Freeman has seen its way °
to publish these matters relating to the private affairs
of one of the members of the Irish Party, that if this
M.P. was content to have his daughter educated in a
convent school in Treland—and there are plenty of
schools even good enough for an Empee’s friend—he
might have escaped this relapse contracted by  without
rest or halt, travelling night and day all the time in
cold and stormy weather.””

A friend has sent us a ¢utting from a paper called
“The Daily Mail,” which is published in London. The
cutting contains matter of some interest to intending =
emigrants to America. Here are some headings:—
“Hard Times in ‘America,”’ “Four More Banks Sus-
pended,’”’ “‘Liabilities £4,800,000,”” ‘““The Exodus to
Europe,” '‘200,000 Workers Returning.”” Other
Leadings are:—‘‘Poverty in Canada,” ‘‘Grave Outlook
for the Coming Winter.””

Ancient Irish art is world-famed, and the Irish sil-
versmiths of to-day have a 'stimulating example in the
specimens of it, which are, fortunately, still preserved.

The Jewellers’ and Silversmiths’ Co., 86 Grafton street,

cffer a variety of Christmas and New Year’s presents, =
which will, in this connection, have a special interest
for Industrial Revivalists. The company have applied
many ancient Irish designs lo articies in modern use,
ranufactured by their own workers. Amongst them we
would like to mention a stationery case made in the
shape of a shrine, the original of which is in the Royal
Irish Academy collection, and a handsome clock, the
case of which is designed from the famous Cross of
Monasterboice.

geweral house repairs, as well as window cleaning, em-

Dublin has well been styled the Jerusalem of
Europe. It is the centre of Jewish culture in the

* westren world, Its population of 600,000, of whom °
00,000 are of the chosen race,its wealth, its com-

&'

THE LEADER,

DeceEMBER 21, 1907.

merce, ' its ‘progress in the fine arts, its seafs of
Jewish learning, famous throughout the world. "All
these are marred by the presence in our widst
of a portion of the old population that so long
rendered the city a barren out-af-elbows town of up-
start poverty and sqﬁulid gentility. The quarter of
the city which Mr. Ernest Isaacs, the distinguished
antiquarian, in his lecture last night before the
“Daniel Society,” to which we direct the attention
of our readers, has identified as th‘e centre of
the city, of pre-Jewish days, still contains no incon-
;iderable population of a mixed breed of the old Gali,
uaels or Gale. And these people, by their squalor,

pudent swagger, by their constant wrangling, by

THOSE MILLIONS IN THE BANKS.

HE money in the Irish banks has been trotted out
T so often—not the money itself, but the fact that
it exists—to round a period for an Industrial Revivalist
who has blossomed into talk, that it reminds us of the
“ No-far-distant Date,”” ‘“The Tide of Emigra-
tion,”” and ‘“The Floor of the British Tlouse
of Commons.”” A few weeks ago Lord Dunraven
trotted out this cry about the money in the banks.
This sort of talk is only clap-trap, it is cheap and dis-
plays lack of thought and incapacity to diagnose.
Talking or making cheaply eloquent speeches about our
millions won’t draw them out of the tills of the banks
and put them into industries to be run on specially emo-
tional and non-business-like lines. Money talks, and
money asks questions ; money, unless it happens to be in
the temporary keeping of a fool, wants to know the
reason why every time.

The banks are really the institutions to be altered,
and they take some altering. As for the individual
depositor in Ireland, he wants, so far as possible, abso-
lute security for his capital sum, a little interest, if he
can get it, and the right to realise his capital at short
notice. He puts his £50, £100, £200, as the case may
be, on deposit in his bank, and he receives a little inter-
est ; hecan draw his money readily ; unlike shares in rail-
ways, so long as his bank is solvent, the £100 he put in
he can take out. Had he put it in a Railway or a Tram-
way Company, it might go up, but it might go down,
and he wants certitude, so far as it is possible to get
it in the financial world; his £100 or £200 is not
worth risking for a rise in a railway share. Besides,
buying shares in existing companies is only going in
in order to let another man out, and is not necessarily
in any way adding to the stock of working national capi-
tal. Perhaps we might qualify this by pointing out that
there is a distinct Anglicisation of capital as of manners
and other things in Ireland. It was only recently we
commented on some Waterford Institution placing, or
agreeing to place, some of its money in British securi-
ties, whilst “ Bog Stock ™ is depressed owing to lack of
support on its merits. It would be a real move in
Ireland if Irish capitalists changed their investments
from British securities to Irish securities to the extent
of taking the partial boycott off Irish securities. The

THE DUBLIN WINTER SHOW.

A LTHOUGH favoured by weather of the most genial

character, the Winter Show failed to attract a big
crowd to Ball’s Bridge. This annual event was re-
luctanly undertaken by the R. D. Society not many
years back, under pressure by influential parties con-
nected with the cattle trade. The R. D. S. are.a
conservative body in more senses than one—they are
very conservative in respect of money. They like to
hear the ““ click, click ”’ of the turnstiles of their shows,
and unless they anticipate a good ‘‘gate’” they fight shy
of such undertakings. ‘‘ Otiun Cum Dignitate *’ is the
motto of the favoured few who fill the nice well-paid
offices under the Society, and beyond the big effort re-
quired in promoting the Spring and the Autumn shows,
these parties do not seem anxious to further exert them-
selves. The Winter Show appears to have always gone
against the grain with the R. D. Society, and a balance
on the wrong side, as a result of the Show, affords a
welcome tangible argument, which they find ready to
their hand as a plea against its further continuance.

Some way or another, the bill of fare provided b7 the
R. D. 8. for the Winter Show was never wholly accept-
able to exhibitors, and except in the section devoted to
cattle, entries in any year did not rule large. The swine
section, in particular, never was a success, and through
long-continued paucity of entries, and consequent lack of
competition experienced at previous Shows, the Society
thought fit to discontinue the swine exhibition, and the
programme this year did not include pigs. Comment
on this state of matters is needless. Imagine the
premier fat stock show of Ireland without a section for
swine—Paddy without his pig! The thing seems an
anomaly. There are frequent organ recitals on the So-
ciety’s premises; a fine display of pedigree cattle at the
Spring Show ; and a big muster of hunters at the Autumn
Show.  There should be a good deal of money in hand
over the latter fixture, and the Society is bound to offer
a better bill of fare for the Winter Show, even though
t,].\ey lost a little over the event. They should, in par-
ticular, make proper arrangements for an exhibition of
Pigs, as an instruction in improved methods of breeding
is so badly needed for Ireland. The English societies
are extending the swine section at their Shows, but the
R. D. 8. has relinquished the section altogether ab its
Winter Show !

To bring out cattle in show form costs something, and,
oven though such cattle usually command a good price,
they hardly ever clear their expenses, except when ‘they

ter advantage inside than outside the show: ring, on ac-

There was nothing very remarkable about the sheep
section.  Numerically, it was but a small show, and, -
through paucity of entries, competition in most of the
classes_was merely nominal. The all round quality:
was fairly good, and some of the exhibits were over the
average as regards general excellence. The long-
woolled varieties were shown together and distinet from
the Downs, which were shown by themselves. Much the
best in the section were a pen of three Roscommon
shearling ewes, the property of Mr. P. J. Fitzmaurice,
Elphin, which scaled 7 cwt. 0qr. 71b (live weight).

Potatoes were shown in rather small quantity, but, as
far as they went, the samples looked fairly good. The
exhibits nearly all belonged to the new varieties—some
only making their appearance the present year. One
of the latter—the Colleen—grown by Mr. J. F. Wil-
liamson, Mallow, Co. Cork, has numerous and flattering
testimonials.  The potatoes were classed as round—
carly and maincrop—and Kidney—early and maincrop
—but this classification was not strictly adhered to, and
in some instances the same variety would appear as an
early in one class, and as a maincrop in another. The
adjudication was somewhat adversely commented on,
particularly in some cases where the award was given to
potatoes which seemed to have no recommendation in
their favour except oversize could be characterised as
such.  As regards roots—mangolds were not up to the
average of former shows, but, it mus’ be recollected, the
season was against them ; turnips made an excellent, and

In the grain section, wheat and oats were but poorly
represented, and chief interest centred in the barley
competition.  There was a big entry from the different
counties, and the judges—Messrs W. H. Hutchinson,
Kircaldy, N.B., and A. E. Walker, Great Yarmouth,
had no easy task in making their awards. The total
amount given in prizes was £290—£10 to £30 to each

ford, Kilkenny, Queen’s, King’s, Tipperary, Waterford,
Cork, and to all others £10. And there were two special
prizes of £10 each, and one of £5. Competition was
confined to each of the counties above named, i.e., to
growers in the county itself, except in the case of the
counties Dublin, Wicklow,Meath and Westmeath, whose
growers jointly competed. The exhibits were classed
Narrow-eared and Wide-eared, respectively. 'The sec-
tion was supported by 289 entries, a proof of the local
interest the exhibition occasioned in the counties
named. There was an interesting competition for the
two champion prizes of £10 each, which were open to all
the counties—Miss Byrne, Killaney, Corcreagh, Co.
Louth, won with Wide-eared; and Mr. P. Scanlon,
Gaill, Cashel, Co. Tipperary, with Narrow-eared. -

Butter made a good display, but the exhibition, taken
all round, was not considered quite equal to the standard
of merit reached last year. ~ Mr. Gibson, the well-
known Limerick expert, acted as judge, and he made
his awards on intrinsic merits of the butter, allow-
ing so many points for aroma, flavour, texture, and
colour—with incidentals—packing and facing.  The
Dairy Societies came out in great form, and took all the
leading honours.

The entries of poultry were less than those of last
year, but the exhibits showed better all-round quality.
The season of year is rather against a poultry exhibition,
as owners do not wish to travel their birds in coid
weather, particularly those of the weaker and more deli-
cate varieties. The largest entries: were of Or-pingtoqs
and Wyandottes. The other breeds were fairly in evi-.
dence, and the laying sorts—the Minorcas and Leg-
horns—commanded most attention. Turkeys, ducks,
and geese made creditable displays; and there was a
small show of dressed poultry, but it should have been
much larger, in view of the increased attention now
Leing given Irish poultry in the Cross-Channel markets.

strongly fortified town, as 'its walls and towers still
testify. Here on the fatal 10th of Augusb', in the year
1316, William Burke and Richard Bermingham gave
battle to Felim O’Connor, King of Connaught, and
slew him and 11,000 of his army. After this dreadful
battle, Athenry grew in importance and seemed for a
time to rival Dublin. Years flew by and the Burkes
became more Irish than the Irish themselves. In
1577 the sons of the Earl of Clanricarde, renouncing
their submission to Elizabeth, attacked the city, sa.ck'ed
it, and left it in ruins. It was rebuilt by English
tradesmen in 1585 and greatly improved. In 1596
O’Donnell’s Northern army invested it, scaled its
walls, set it on fire, and, with the exception of the
Castle and Abbey, left it once more a heap of ashes.
Athenry never fully recovered its former glory. In
Cromwell’s days its noble Monasteries were despoiled
and their inmates hunted like wild beasts. Some of
these died martyrs, such as the Dominican Fathers,
Dillon and Petit, and three of their lay brothers,
namely, brothers Moran, Black and Hovedon. The
banished Dominicans kept the lamp of faith unquenched
amid the bogs and woods that surround Athenry.
About the year 1650, they opened a school at Esker.
This school numbered 300 students in the year 1665.
The students built their own cells of boards and
wattles, and received their lectures in the open spaces
of the woods. They got food as best they could; it
was sent to them chiefly by their parents from all
parts of Ireland. Great lawyers, doctors, and learned
priests and bishops were educated in Athenry and
Esker. Many of them rose to high rank on the Con-
tinent.

So famous was this school of Athenry and Esker that.
the Sovereign Pontiff raised it to the dignity of a
University. Alas, the times were too evil, and the
goodwill of the Pope could not be carried into effect.
Persecution again scattered the students; but the
school lingered on in spite of all troubles till it came,
finally, after many vicissitudes, into the hands of the:
Redemptorist Fathers. Once more students, the sons. °
of St. Alphonsus, walk those places hallowed during
ages by the prayers and even by the blood of. the:
faithful sons of St. Dominic and their disciples.

and would afford materials for a great epic; for West
of it lies the great plain of Moytura where for five
days the Belgae and the Danaans fought for the crown.
of Erin.

brethren of the Order, and tell their Catholic comrade

wants. The trail of the Freemasons is over the whole
police establishment.  The R.I.C. should urge these
reforms by every legitimate means in their power on the

LESSON.

3}‘ oUu ungrudgingly gave so much of your space to
the discussion “of the Library -Question a year
2go, that I am loath to trespass on your generosity in
connection with ~the same subject again. But having
Ventured at the time on an anticipation in regard to an
_ mportant practical experiment, then in its initial
stages, in the matter of Rural Free Libraries, your
readers who followed the discussion may be interested to
know what the issue has been. Z
Writing in the LeADER of January 19th, 1907, I
said :— : >
The Rural District Council most forward in availing themselves
of the Carnegic grant is, I beliceve, that of Rathkeale, Co. Limerick.
In the Aunster News, of the 22nd December, which I havo before
mo as I write, there is a report of a meeting of this Free Library
Committee.  From the proccedings it transpires that the Rato
made for Free Libraries in tho Union is £145, of which it is
cstimated that £95 will bo swallowed up in management and
administration expenses, leaving £50 for the purchase of books.
This does not look quite so bad on the face of it. But when one
of oxperience in such matters comes to analyse the items he can
arrive at no other conclusion than that in a few years, if not in
the very first, the expenses will absorb the entire rate made; and
this must bo doubled if the whole project is not to collapse. = The
items in question are—<£20 for librarian at Rathkeale (for which
post there will probably be as much canvassing and competition
as for a municipal job running into four figures); £5 each for
eight district librarians (who must soon, and only in bare justice,
be graded up towards the salary of the Rathkealo one, or elso
there must be a levelling down); £1 each for National Schools—of
which there are, presumably, 10, as the amount loft for other ex-
penses is put down at £25. These “other expenses include, T
take it, fire and light for nino library buildings; the up-keep of
ths Structures themselves; carriage of books for periodical ex-
change between the libraries, over a circuit of more than 50 miles
from start to finish; and remuneration for secretarial work. Can
any sane man believe that even twice £25 will cover thewe
“other expenses ” ?

Well, one year has not quite gone by, and from
some lively discussions (reported at great length in
Tecent issues of the ‘‘ Munster News’’) which have
taken place at three successive meetings of the Rath-
keale District Council, it transpires that unless an
additional annual grant of from £30 to £36 is made to
the Central Library at Rathkeale, the institution will
not open its doors at all. And the humour of it is,
the Committee has for a considerable time been paying
a librarian, whose office, so far, appears to be a sine-
cure.. The District Council refused to make this grant
of £30 to £36, as it would only leave a balance of from
£15 to £20 for the purchase of books for nine libraries.
And, further, the most active and experienced member
of the Council—an ex-Lord Mayor of Dublin—gave
nctice that he would at next meeting move that the Dis-
trict Library Rate be discontinued, as it would prac-
tically. be all swallowed up in salaries and administrative
expenses generally. And so the Rathkeale Committee,
with their white elephant of a paid librarian on hands,
for, I believe, nearly a year, decided at a late meeting,
by a formal vote of 8 to 2 not to open. For the

_ secretarial work in connection with the Rathkeale Rural
Library project, the clerk of the Council gets £20 per
annum, and his assistant £5, and the former stated
positively at the last meeting that if this remuneration

- were stopped he would mnot discharge the duties.

Parenthetically, I may remark that I am acquainted
with -a Free Library which expends about £100 per
aunum in the purchase of books and papers, etc. The
duties of secretary are discharged gratuitously by a
local official in a position analogous to that of the
Rathkeale clerk, who, on being offered suitable re-
muneration, refused it on the grounds that if members
of the Committee in no better position than himself
were making sacrifice of time and trouble in a laudable
project, he felt it incubent on him to do the same.

The foregoing is the first part, so to speak, of my
object lesson ; and now for the second. In the Leaper
of December 22nd, 1906, T wrote as follows:—

~ [ And T question, as a practical and oxpericnced man of busi-

ness, whether tho Parochial Library would not bo cheaper—ns
well as being far more efficient—than tho Free, Carnegio one. In

_ the Parochial you will have the supervision and management.
iven, 08 o rulo, gratuitously, and the books perhaps housed rent-

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 21, 1907.

freo, while in the Carnegic or other public Library the administra-
tion expenses are suro to bo on the vory highest scals, and may,
particularly in o rural district, absorb the entirc rato of even
pouny in the pound. I know of ono instance myself where, in the
caso of o large corporato town, it was, after the fullest considera-
tion, found cheaper to run a library independently of the Carnegio
grant than under the conditions which would accompany this.

And in proof of the opinion thus ventured by me a
year ago, I now beg to cite the following :—Newcastle
West is situated some six miles from Rathkeale, and
has about the same population—2,000 odd. Eighteen
months ago, or less, an attempt was made there to in-
troduce the Carnegie Free (?) Library project on similar
lines to Rathkeale. This was opposed before the Dis-
trict Council by the enlightened pastor, Monsignor
Hallinan, D:D., V.G., who knew full well the grave
objection from a financial—not to mind a higher—
paint of view to the proposal, and who, besides, assisted
by a representative Committee of local laymen, had for
years maintained an efficient parochial library in the
town. The result was that the scheme was not adopted.

Monsignor Hallinan incurred considerable odium in
anti-clerical quarters for the stand he took on the
occasion; and the dictionary would appear to have
been ransacked for epithets to characterize his ‘‘ob-
scurantist >’ and ‘‘ reactionary’’ conduct. But ‘‘he
laughs best- who laughs last,”” and the Monsigner’s
vindication has come even more quickly than could well
be expected.

Synchronizing, I may say, with the late discussions
at the Rathkeale District Council, already adverted to,
the annual meeting of the Newcastle West Parochial
Library Committee took place. Amongst those present
(as reported in the ‘‘ Munster News’’) were the parish
priest and one of his curates, the local national teacher,
the Chairman of the Town Commissioners, two magis-
trates also County Councillors—one of them Chairman
of the District Council—and other responsible and
representative men.

From the report it would appear that the library was
well stocked with works on all subiects of interest—
poetry, biography, history, travel, and science—and
dealing with every phase of Irish life—literary, ccono-
mic, social, political, and religious—together with a
good supply of fiction by standard writers. There was
a balance of £23 odd to credit—fifty per cent. more
money, it will be observed, than the Carnegie people at
Rathkeale could command to purchase books for nine
libraries, unless their central one were ket closed.
Monsignor Hallinan, in addressing the Committee, said,
amongst other things:—‘‘ We are no burden to others
or to the ratepayers at large. Experience teaches that
public libraries in the very few rural districts in which
they have been introduced are no boon. They are a
waste of public money, the cost of administration prac-
tically swallowing up the rate, and leaving little, if
any margin for the purchase of books.”

It would, of course, be out of the question to expect
that those discinles of Tolstoy and Karl Marx—bye-
products of the bastard system of higher education with
which we have so long been afflicted—who a year back
or so spent their vituperation on Monsignor Hallinan,
will profit in any way by the lesson on the library
question afforded by the humiliating fiasco at Rath-
keale and the signal success at Newcastle West. They
will go on as ever, ignorantly and self-sufficiently dog-
matising and dictating on the affairs of the country in
general—social and political, religious and educational.
But it is earnestly to be hoped that the lesson will not
be lost on the tax-paying and long-suffering public.
